children.name: Diomedea
children.description: The great albatrosses — the largest seabirds on Earth, with the longest wingspans of any living bird. The Wandering Albatross can reach a wingspan of 3.7 metres. These ocean wanderers spend most of their lives in flight, covering thousands of kilometres over the Southern Ocean using dynamic soaring. They return to land only to breed, forming lifelong pair bonds.

children.name: Diomedea exulans
children.description: The bird with the longest wingspan on Earth, reaching 3.7 metres. White body with black wingtips. Breeds on subantarctic islands including South Georgia. Spends years at sea without touching land, gliding effortlessly over Southern Ocean swells. Its life expectancy can exceed 50 years.

children.name: Diomedea epomophora
children.description: Very similar to the Wandering Albatross but with white wingtips and a smaller bill. Breeds on the subantarctic islands of New Zealand, particularly Campbell Island and the Auckland Islands.

children.name: Diomedea amsterdamensis
children.description: One of the rarest albatrosses, with a breeding population restricted to Amsterdam Island in the southern Indian Ocean. The entire population numbers fewer than 150 individuals. Classified as Critically Endangered.

children.name: Diomedea sanfordi
children.description: A royal albatross breeding on the Chatham Islands and Otago Peninsula.

children.name: Diomedea antipodensis
children.description: A great albatross from the Antipodes Islands.

children.name: Diomedea gibsoni
children.description: A great albatross from the Auckland Islands.

children.name: Thalassarche
children.description: The mollymawks — medium-sized albatrosses with black-and-white plumage and brightly coloured bills. They are the most numerous and widespread albatrosses, often following ships for extended periods. Several species are threatened by longline fishing.

children.name: Thalassarche melanophris
children.description: The most common and widespread albatross, with an estimated 1.2 million individuals. White body, dark grey back, and a distinctive dark eyebrow. Breeds in colonies on Falkland Islands, South Georgia, and other subantarctic islands. Regularly seen in the North Atlantic, with records off the British and Swedish coasts.

children.name: Thalassarche cauta
children.description: A large mollymawk breeding on islands off Tasmania and New Zealand. Grey body with a white face and a distinctive yellow bill. Classified as Near Threatened. Known for its timid behaviour around boats, hence the name.

children.name: Thalassarche chlororhynchos
children.description: A small albatross breeding on Tristan da Cunha and Gough Island in the South Atlantic. Black bill with a bright yellow ridge. The only albatross that regularly occurs in the North Atlantic, with rare records off European coasts.

children.name: Thalassarche bulleri
children.description: A mollymawk with grey head and bright yellow bill from New Zealand.

children.name: Thalassarche impavida
children.description: A mollymawk breeding only on Campbell Island, New Zealand.

children.name: Thalassarche eremita
children.description: A mollymawk breeding only on the Chatham Islands.

children.name: Thalassarche salvini
children.description: A mollymawk from the Bounty and Snares Islands.

children.name: Thalassarche steadi
children.description: A mollymawk from the Auckland Islands.

children.name: Thalassarche bulleri
children.description: A mollymawk from islands around New Zealand.

children.name: Thalassarche impavida
children.description: A mollymawk breeding only on Campbell Island.

children.name: Thalassarche steadi
children.description: A mollymawk from the Auckland Islands.

children.name: Thalassarche salvini
children.description: A mollymawk from the Bounty Islands.

children.name: Thalassarche eremita
children.description: A mollymawk from the Chatham Islands.

children.name: Phoebetria
children.description: Sooty albatrosses — distinctive dark-plumaged albatrosses with long, wedge-shaped tails and a more gull-like flight. Found exclusively in the Southern Ocean. Their dark plumage may serve as camouflage against the dark seas of the stormy latitudes they inhabit.

children.name: Phoebetria fusca
children.description: A dark sooty-brown albatross with a distinctive yellow stripe on the bill. Breeds on subantarctic islands in the South Atlantic and Indian Oceans. More agile in flight than the great albatrosses, with a distinctive gliding and banking style.

children.name: Phoebetria palpebrata
children.description: A sooty albatross with pale grey back from the Southern Ocean.
